items.title: ADA and WHO diagnostic criteria
items.contentHtml:
ADA and WHO diagnostic criteria

  • American Diabetes Association (ADA) and World Health Organization (WHO) share 4 criteria: fasting plasma glucose ≥126 mg/dL, 2-hour plasma glucose in oral glucose tolerance test (75 g) ≥200 mg/dL, glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) ≥6.5% (NGSP/DCCT standardized), or classic symptoms of hyperglycemia with random plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL.
  • For confirmation, repeat the test on another sample unless there is unequivocal hyperglycemia; two abnormal tests (e.g., HbA1c ≥6.5% + fasting ≥126 mg/dL) confirm the diagnosis.
  • The WHO does not recommend HbA1c as the sole criterion in certain populations (anemia, hemoglobinopathies, pregnancy).

items.title: Type 1, type 2 and gestational classification
items.contentHtml:
Type 1, type 2 and gestational classification

  • Type 1 diabetes mellitus: autoimmune destruction of beta cells (presence of autoantibodies: anti-GAD, anti-IA2, anti-insulin, anti-ZnT8), absolute insulin deficiency, tendency to ketosis, acute onset at any age, but classically in children/young people; requires lifelong insulin therapy.
  • Type 2 diabetes mellitus: insulin resistance with relative insulin deficiency, without autoantibodies, associated with obesity/sedentary lifestyle, insidious evolution; stepwise treatment: lifestyle changes, metformin, other hypoglycemic agents, insulin therapy.
  • Gestational diabetes mellitus: hyperglycemia diagnosed for the first time in the second/third trimester of pregnancy, no previous overt diabetes; screening with 75 g oral glucose tolerance test between weeks 24-28, with specific cut-off points (fasting ≥92 mg/dL, 1 hour ≥180 mg/dL, 2 hours ≥153 mg/dL).

items.title: Insulin resistance pathophysiology
items.contentHtml:
Pathophysiology of insulin resistance

  • Insulin resistance is the decreased ability of target tissues (skeletal muscle, liver, adipose tissue) to respond to insulin; mechanisms: defect in postreceptor signaling (phosphorylation of the insulin receptor substrate IRS-1), lipotoxicity due to free fatty acids, chronic inflammation (tumor necrosis factor alpha, interleukin-6).
  • In the muscle: lower translocation of glucose transporter type 4 (GLUT4) to the membrane. In the liver: increased gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis, with lack of suppression of hepatic glucose production by insulin.
  • visceral adiposity releases proinflammatory cytokines and free fatty acids that aggravate insulin resistance; adiponectin (insulin sensitive) is decreased.

items.title: diabetic retinopathy
items.contentHtml:
Diabetic retinopathy

  • Diabetic retinopathy is the most common microvascular complication; classification: non-proliferative (microaneurysms, hard exudates, intraretinal hemorrhages, cotton wool spots) and proliferative (neovascularization, vitreous hemorrhage, tractional retinal detachment).
  • Diabetic macular edema is the main cause of visual loss; It is diagnosed with optical coherence tomography (OCT).
  • Screening: dilated fundus at diagnosis in type 2 diabetes mellitus, 5 years after the onset of type 1 diabetes mellitus (if age ≥10 years); then annual if normal, more frequent if altered.
  • Treatment: strict glycemic and hypertensive control; for macular or proliferative edema: laser photocoagulation (panretinal in proliferative), anti-intravitreal vascular endothelial growth factor (ranibizumab, aflibercept).

items.title: Coronary heart disease in diabetes
items.contentHtml:
Coronary heart disease in diabetes mellitus

  • Type 2 diabetes mellitus confers a cardiovascular risk equivalent to established coronary heart disease (it is considered coronary risk equivalent); Coronary artery disease in diabetes is often diffuse, multivessel and silent (silent myocardial ischemia due to autonomic neuropathy).
  • Contributing factors: diabetic dyslipidemia (elevated triglycerides, low high-density lipoprotein cholesterol, small and dense low-density lipoprotein particles), hypertension, hyperglycemia, insulin resistance, inflammation.
  • Prevention: intensive control of risk factors (target blood pressure <130/80 mmHg, low-density lipoprotein cholesterol <55 mg/dL at very high risk); use of statins (see specific focus) and antiplatelet agents if secondary prevention.

items.title: Acute management of diabetic ketoacidosis
items.contentHtml:
Acute management of diabetic ketoacidosis

  • Diagnostic criteria: blood glucose ≥250 mg/dL, pH <7.3, bicarbonate <15 mEq/L, ketonemia (ketonuria), elevated anion gap (>12).
  • Management: 1) Volume replacement: isotonic saline solution 0.9% 15-20 mL/kg in the first hour; then adjust according to hemodynamic status and corrected sodium. 2) Intravenous regular insulin: bolus of 0.1 units/kg followed by infusion of 0.1 units/kg/hour; when blood glucose <200 mg/dL, reduce to 0.05 units/kg/hour and add 5-10% dextrose to avoid hypoglycemia. 3) Correct potassium: if potassium <3.3 mEq/L, replace before insulin; maintain potassium between 4-5 mEq/L. 4) Bicarbonate: only if pH <6.9 (in adults).
  • Monitor every hour: blood glucose, electrolytes, pH, anion gap, ketones; look for a trigger (infection, insulin omission, heart attack).

items.title: Hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state
items.contentHtml:
Hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state

  • Characteristics: blood glucose >600 mg/dL, effective plasma osmolarity >320 mOsm/kg, pH >7.3, bicarbonate >15 mEq/L, absent or mild ketonuria; typical in type 2 diabetes mellitus, with severe dehydration and altered sensorium.
  • Management: volume replacement with 0.9% isotonic saline solution (similar to diabetic ketoacidosis, but the water deficit is greater, up to 8-10 L); regular intravenous insulin at lower doses (bolus 0.05-0.1 units/kg, infusion 0.05 units/kg/hour) because they are more sensitive to insulin; correct potassium and sodium according to level.
  • Complications: thromboembolism (prophylaxis with low molecular weight heparin), cerebral edema (rare, more in children), rhabdomyolysis; Mortality is higher than in diabetic ketoacidosis.

items.title: Metformin as first line
items.contentHtml:
Metformin as first line

  • Metformin is the first-line drug in type 2 diabetes mellitus (unless contraindicated), it belongs to the biguanides; mechanism: decreases hepatic glucose production (gluconeogenesis) and improves peripheral insulin sensitivity, does not stimulate insulin secretion (low risk of hypoglycemia).
  • Initial dose: 500 mg every 12 hours or 850 mg daily with meals, gradually increase to maximum dose 2550 mg/day (in the United States 2000 mg/day).
  • Adverse effects: gastrointestinal (nausea, diarrhea, metallic taste) that They improve with dose adjustment and administration with food; risk of lactic acidosis (rare, 0.03 cases/1000 patient-years), contraindicated if estimated glomerular filtration rate <30 mL/min/1.73 m² (in Europe <45, adjust dose if between 30-45).

items.title: Insulin therapy in type 1 diabetes
items.contentHtml:
Insulin therapy in type 1 diabetes

  • In type 1 diabetes mellitus, insulin therapy is for life; basal-bolus scheme: long-acting insulin (basal analogue: glargine or detemir or degludec) 1-2 times/day + rapid-acting insulin (analogue: lispro, aspart, glulisine) before each meal; total daily dose 0.4-1.0 units/kg/day (50% basal, 50% prandial).
  • Alternative: continuous subcutaneous infusion of insulin (insulin pump) with rapid analogue; adjustment according to capillary blood glucose, carbohydrate intake and physical activity.
  • Avoid hypoglycemia (blood glucose <70 mg/dL): rule of 15 (15 g of simple carbohydrates, repeat blood glucose after 15 minutes); if severe hypoglycemia with altered sensory: intramuscular/subcutaneous glucagon (adults 1 mg) or intravenous dextrose.

items.foco: Screening and management of diabetic foot
items.title: Screening and management of diabetic foot
items.contentHtml:
Screening and management of diabetic foot

  • Annual screening: foot inspection (fissures, calluses, ulcers, deformities), evaluation of protective sensitivity with 10 g monofilament (points: first toe, metatarsal heads 1, 3 and 5; if not perceived in ≥1 point, risk of neuropathy), Achilles reflex, pedis and posterior tibial pulses.
  • Ulcer classification: Wagner system (grade 0: intact skin; grade 1: superficial ulcer; grade 2: deep; grade 3: abscess/osteomyelitis; grade 4: limited gangrene; grade 5: extensive gangrene).
  • Management of neuropathic ulcer: discharge (total contact cast, removable splint), debridement, infection control (antibiotic directed at bone culture if osteomyelitis), revascularization if peripheral arterial disease; amputation is the last resort.

items.title: Perioperative glycemic control
items.contentHtml:
Perioperative glycemic control

  • Perioperative goal: blood glucose between 100-180 mg/dL (avoid extreme hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia); major procedures: discontinue metformin and other oral hypoglycemic agents 24-48 hours before (risk of lactic acidosis with metformin if acute renal failure).
  • In type 1 diabetes mellitus: do not suspend basal insulin (risk of diabetic ketoacidosis); reduce basal insulin dose the night before (e.g. glargine 80% of the usual dose).
  • During surgery: intravenous regular insulin infusion (0.5-1.0 units/hour) + 5% dextrose if blood glucose <180 mg/dL; monitor blood glucose every 1-2 hours; postoperative: resume usual regimen when the patient tolerates oral administration.

items.foco: Criteria for starting statins in DM
items.title: Criteria for starting statins in diabetes mellitus
items.contentHtml:
Criteria for starting statins in diabetes mellitus

  • Statins are indicated in type 2 diabetes mellitus for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular events; according to American Diabetes Association (ADA) guidelines 2024: in patients with diabetes mellitus and established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, start high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40-80 mg/day or rosuvastatin 20-40 mg/day) to reduce low-density lipoprotein cholesterol ≥50% and target <55 mg/dL.
  • In prevention primary: if age 40-75 years without atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, start moderate intensity statin (atorvastatin 10-20 mg/day, rosuvastatin 5-10 mg/day) if low-density lipoprotein cholesterol ≥70 mg/dL or additional risk factors; if age >75 years, consider risk/benefit.
  • In type 1 diabetes mellitus with risk factors or age ≥40 years, apply the same criteria.
